candidate_problems.text: Can the genealogical diagnosis be turned into a substantive alternative theory rather than a debunking story? The central unresolved tension is whether showing that the information/phenomenality divide is historically inherited actually changes the ontology or explanatory task, rather than merely explaining why we talk this way. What new positive concepts, models, or problem-statements replace the current ones, and how would they generate testable or criticizable progress on consciousness?

Which features of phenomenal consciousness were genuinely excluded by the computational/information-processing framework, and which are only described that way because of inherited assumptions? This is an open question about conceptual decomposition: are subjectivity, perspectivalness, qualitative character, unity, and first-person accessibility all artifacts of a historical vocabulary, or do some mark real explanatory constraints any adequate theory must capture?

How should 'information' be reconceived if the current notion was historically optimized for publicly characterizable functional organization? The unresolved problem is to formulate a richer concept that can include subjectivity without collapsing into vagueness, dualism, or trivial relabeling. What structural changes to the concept of information would preserve mechanistic explanatory power while no longer defining consciousness out from the start?
